turns.text: Provide a new formulation of only a separate articulation which I can prepend to the prompt as background context that adequate described the intents/purposes/goals/etc. behind our working on what we are working on, with various levels of scope/specificity being adequately contextualized/articulated. We want to have this so that we do not lose sight of the overlying objectibes, such as what we I have contextualizd before through the providing of previous chat/session content/archives/etc.

Use this as a prependable background-context block ahead of the operational prompt:

[code: unknown]

code_blocks.code:
BACKGROUND CONTEXT / OVERLYING OBJECTIVES

This work exists within a broader document-processing and ingestion objective: ensure that semantically important information contained in source documents survives processing in a form that is materially useful downstream, even when that information is not adequately recoverable through OCR/text extraction alone.

At the broadest scope, the overarching objective is not merely “extract text,” but to preserve document meaning and utility across heterogeneous content types. That includes ordinary text-bearing pages, mixed text/visual pages, and pages whose critical informational value is carried primarily by dense visual structure (for example diagrams, schematics, complex figures, detail-heavy image content, or other OCR-insufficient page content). The broader system objective is therefore fidelity of retained information, not just maximization of extracted text.

At the subsystem scope, the relevant area is the local PDF/document-processing path responsible for turning source pages into usable processing outputs. Within that scope, the central concern is whether the current local processing path can correctly distinguish between content that is adequately handled by existing text-oriented extraction and content that requires an additional visual-preservation path. The goal is to avoid silent semantic loss for detail-heavy visual pages while preserving existing functionality for ordinary and OCR-sufficient content.

At the current lane scope, the specific objective is bounded and local: determine and, if justified, implement what is necessary so that preserve-eligible visual pages are handled in a directly visible, locally testable way. Concretely, this means establishing or proving a local result/output surface that supports:
- preserve-eligible visual-page success behavior,
- non-fatal visual-artifact failure behavior,
- and explicit skip/no-op behavior for non-preserve pages (such as `text_heavy_or_empty`),
all without requiring downstream integration to prove that the local lane itself is working.

This lane is intentionally narrower than broader pipeline completion. Its purpose is to establish a correct and testable local foundation. It is not, by itself, meant to prove end-to-end persistence, downstream consumer behavior, staging-table population, full multimodal handling, or broad integration completeness. Those are adjacent or later concerns and should remain explicitly outside proof unless directly brought into scope.

Historical chat/session outputs, prior archives, and previously reconstructed states are relevant as orientation for the overlying objectives and prior intent, but they are not authoritative over the current canonical repo state. They should be used to preserve awareness of the broader purpose of the work, not to override what the current codebase directly proves.

The practical success condition for this lane is therefore twofold:
1. preserve the already-correct local processing behavior that should remain intact, and
2. ensure that detail-heavy / OCR-insufficient visual-content handling is either correctly proven present or correctly implemented in a bounded way, with explicit local success/failure/skip semantics and direct testability.

The intended implementation philosophy for this lane is also bounded: prefer additive, explicit, minimally coupled, page-level behavior that is reasonably robust, testable, and extendable, without drifting into broader redesign. The goal is not maximal change; it is reliable local functionality that closes a real semantic gap while remaining coherent with the broader document-ingestion objective.

turns.text: Are there any questions I can answer right now about how I want the approach/handling of the detail heavy/non-OCR-able images/image artifacts/PDF pages/etc., from beginning to end of the ingestion/processing (from top to farthest down stream)

Yes. There are several decisions that would collapse a lot of ambiguity before any more implementation.
The key assumption is that you do not just want “some image pages captured,” but a stable end-to-end contract: detection, representation, storage/reference, downstream interpretation, and retrieval/use. The main risk right now is building a locally working artifact path that later conflicts with how you actually want those pages to behave downstream.
The highest-value questions are the ones that define the contract across the whole path, not just the first extraction step.
1. Detection / routing

What should count as a “detail-heavy / OCR-insufficient” page?

only pages with near-useless OCR?

pages where OCR exists but loses critical structure?

only fully image-dominant pages?

also mixed text+diagram pages?

Should detection be:

heuristic/rule-based,

classifier-label-based,

manually thresholded,

or permissive at first and tightened later?

What is the unit of routing?

whole page only,

page region/crop,

both eventually, but page-only first?

When in doubt, should the system:

preserve too much,

preserve too little,

or mark uncertain pages for a conservative visual-preservation path?

2. Representation / artifact form

What is the canonical preserved form for these pages?

PNG

JPEG

rendered full-page image plus metadata

some structured artifact wrapper containing image + metadata

Is the artifact meant to represent:

the full page,

only the visually important region,

or full page first, with region support later?

Do you want one artifact per qualifying page, or can a page produce multiple artifacts?

Should OCR text and visual artifact both coexist for the same page, or should visual preservation replace text for certain pages?

3. Output contract from the local processing stage

What exact output surface do you want from the local processing stage?

refs only

refs + failures

refs + failures + page-level metadata

refs + failures + page-level classification/routing reason

What minimum metadata must travel with each preserved visual page?

source file path / document id

page number

page index

visual class / routing reason

ordering among other pages

link to OCR/text units from same page

ingestion run id

artifact storage location

Do you want failures surfaced:

per page, non-fatally,

aggregated at document level,

or both?

What should happen for skip/no-op pages like text_heavy_or_empty?

silently skip

skip but record why

skip with explicit page-level status in output

4. Storage / referencing

Where do you want the preserved visual artifacts to live conceptually?

local artifact directory

object storage

sidecar files near ingestion artifacts

abstract URI/pointer interface, storage backend hidden

Should downstream systems receive:

direct file paths,

stable artifact IDs,

URIs,

or structured page records containing refs?

Is artifact naming/path structure important now?

page-number-based naming

content-hash-based naming

run-specific ephemeral naming

document-stable naming

Do you care about deduplication now, or is that explicitly out of scope?

5. Downstream interpretation / use

What should downstream components do with these visual artifacts?

simply retain them for later retrieval

send them to a multimodal model

include them in chunking/indexing

keep them as sidecar evidence only

Should downstream retrieval/search treat these artifacts as:

first-class retrievable units,

attachments to text chunks,

page-level auxiliary context,

not searchable yet, only preserved

If a page has both OCR text and a visual artifact, what is the downstream semantic relationship?

text is primary, artifact is supporting

artifact is primary, text is supporting

both are equal modalities attached to the same page record

Do you eventually want these pages to influence document classification, quality scoring, or chunk generation downstream?

6. Product / quality bar

What is the minimum outcome that would make you say this lane is “working”?

qualifying pages are preserved

preserved pages are returned with stable refs

failures are non-fatal and visible

downstream stage can consume them

end-to-end retrieval/use is proven

What is explicitly acceptable for the first completed version?

page-level only

no region crops yet

local refs only

no downstream consumer yet

deterministic tests only

What is explicitly unacceptable even in a first version?

hidden side effects

silent failures

no provenance

brittle path conventions

behavior only provable via integration, not local tests

7. Risk / policy tradeoffs

Which mistake is worse?

false positives: preserving too many pages visually

false negatives: failing to preserve important visual pages

Should the system optimize first for:

fidelity,

speed,

storage efficiency,

downstream simplicity,

implementation simplicity

Is there any content type that should be excluded even if image-heavy?

scanned signatures

decorative covers

repeated logos

appendices

photos without informational density

8. Sequence / roadmap

What order do you want this built in?

detect → preserve → expose refs → test locally

detect → preserve → downstream consume

preserve first for all candidates, refine detection later

Do you want the current lane to stop at local proof of handling, or should it include one downstream consumer step before you consider it complete?

Is the next lane after this supposed to be:

downstream propagation

retrieval/indexing semantics

multimodal consumption

region-level refinement

Highest-leverage questions to answer first
If you only answer a few now, these matter most:

Should the handling unit be full page only for now, or do you want region/crop support in scope now?

For qualifying pages, should the system preserve both OCR text and visual artifact, or should visual preservation become the primary representation?

What exact result/output surface do you want from local processing: refs only, refs+failures, or refs+failures+page metadata?

What exact metadata is mandatory for every preserved visual page?

Should downstream receive file paths, artifact IDs, URIs, or structured page records?

What is the minimum bar for saying the lane works: local proof only, or one downstream consumer proven too?

Which error is worse: false positives or false negatives in routing pages to the visual-preservation path?

If you answer those seven, the next implementation/test prompt can be much tighter and less guessy.
